author: @ryanjamesloyd6733
text:
This reminds me of a thing called a Lorena stove-  A buddy of mine named Tap was very popular in the villages in Africa (he was peace corps) because he knew how to build one out of compacted clay and sand, and it's a game changer for cooking in terms of ease and fuel consumption over an open fire.  You can even bake in it.  It seems to me, that instead of a cooking grid on top, one could construct an easy 2 pot Lorena stove on a table over the rocket stove (where the firebox for the lorena would usually be) and it would increase that fuel efficiency further. 

This could be inline with the sand battery as well- if you packed the body of your rocket stove in sand, you could capture That heat, which would otherwise be lost to the air,  for other use without impacting the cooking function.  (heating water, running a stirling, home heating, whatever)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: good ideas mate - thanks for sharing

author: @ravenrock541
text:
Your design is an L with a third leg at 45 deg. It's convenient for auto feeding the fuel, but it doesn't burn clean. My rocket stove is a true L shape, made from 3/8 inch thick tubular steel. The horizontal leg of the L gives time for the fuel to completely combust and crack the gaseous hydrocarbons (the thicker steel also increases the combustion areas heat which helps, I am sure). I can cook on the horizontal leg of the L when I want indirect heat (or am burning fuel not suitable for getting on the food) plus cook on the vertical part of the L.
   It burns so clean I can use used motor oil as fuel without any smoke. It's not something I do as a practice, but I was curious.
Side note: I found that the vertical leg of the L needs to be more than 3 times the length of the  horizontal leg.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice pointers mate and thank you

replies.author: @islesanctum833
replies.text:
Taller leg yes
45,° works well with no down side at all
Perfect over "L" shape
My experience cooking almost daily for over 8 months

replies.author: @frederickwood9116
@ravenrock541
replies.text: @frederickwood9116 When I built mine I had two requirements; it had to burn light soft wood (plentiful in my area) and hide the flame and smoke from sight (pesky neighbors). Mine is a straight square tube L. Air is drawn in where the opening is (fuel is also added here, as well) at the end of the horizontal portion of the L. Open flame takes place inside the tube here. As the fuel turns to coals it is pushed further into the tube by new fuel. The coal area usually ends up about 6-9 inches. As the smoke from the open flame area flows over the coals the heat cracks the hydrocarbons in the smoke (read unburnt fuel) and then adds to the combustion. I get a good draft thru the stove, so it is my belief that the time it takes for the smoke to travel across the coals in the horizontal area of the L is what gives me a really clean burn (no smoke). This stove design requires that you baby sit the fire and feed it fuel every 15 min, but I don't mind that. My horizontal portion of the L is long enough that I can set a large stew pot on it. I get a nice uniform heat this way, it is also low enough that burning the food is not an issue, but is hot). Also, I can burn any wood I want without worrying about it getting into my food. I made a fixture to set onto the top of the horizontal portion of the L over the exhaust opening so I can use pot there as well if I choose, but I rarely do. The stove requires small hand sized pieces of wood, but once I have a good bed of coals I can stuff it as much as I desire. I'll send a pic later, but as you can see, it's basic simple. Square tube cut to create an L. The horizontal portion of the L is the combustion area and the vertical is the chimney. It the ratio of length between these two that really matters. I found the chimney needs to be 3 times longer than the combustion area to get a decent draft.

replies.author: @frederickwood9116

author: @totherarf
text:
Great topic ..... Man releases the boy in him and does good!

A side issue on the rockets though. People say how efficient they are (even if you do have to babysit them). 
My question is .... do you get more heat out of one of them as against an A rated wood stove? Is it the same, but quicker?

replies.author: @rayg436
replies.text:
Totally different with the rocket stove you are cooking with the exhaust that a conventional wood stove sends out of the building .
So much more efficient.

replies.author: @totherarf
replies.text: @rayg436 Does that mean your Rocket Mass heater is less efficient than your stove?

replies.author: @rayg436
replies.text: @totherarf no because a rocket stove burns more efficient and hotter. Burns most of the sutt and smoke as fuel. The heat produced in a mass heater is trapped in the mass and slowly released.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate and I really don't know the answer to that - though I have arguments from all sides!

replies.author: @totherarf
replies.text:
@rayg436 I understand your point, and you may be right. 

I have a wood burning fire. It is a Class A type which means that pyrolyses the wood and then burns the gasses. This leaves less than a cup full of very fine ash after a nights burn and once it heats up (which does not take long) it produces no visible smoke. Also the exhaust gas goes up a chimney that gets warm, even in the bedroom above! 

Obviously you can't use the rocket cook stove inside, but converted into a mass heater it can be and people rave about them. They burn hot and quick, which is a problem if you want to heat a home up with one, but is great if you only want to cook your dinner. 
My question is which method gets more out of a set amount of fuel, or is there little difference? The uses are different and that means it is hard to compare apples with apples. 
If I were building a house and had free reign over design would I be better with a rocket mass heater or a good design of standard wood burner?

author: @markbothum4338
text: It was 95 degrees here (Alaska) in 2019, so too hot to cook indoors, since air conditioning is virtually unheard of here, AND we had a burn ban. So I built a quick and dirty rocket stove in about 5 minutes from a half-dozen foundation bricks (plenty of online videos) and it worked pretty well. Kind of sooted up the pan bottoms, so maybe don't use the wife's best T-Fals. (Oops)

replies.author: @totherarf

Once you finish simply wipe clean with a paper towel. Simples! 
Don't ask how I found that one out ;o)
